pages.text: The stars over Bengaluru glowed brighter than usual the night <name> got the message. Her wrist communicator beeped softly — ping! — and a hologram of ISRO’s Young Explorers emblem shimmered above her bed. “<name> Iyer,” said the voice of Dr. Meera, the team’s leader. “You’ve been selected for the Starlight Mission. Report to Space Command at dawn.”

<name> gasped. She had dreamed of this day since she first watched rockets soar from Sriharikota on TV with Papa. “Amma! I’m going to space!” she cried, hugging her mother, who smiled proudly and handed her a box of warm parathas. “Even space travelers need real food,” Amma said.

By morning, <name> stood before the sleek silver spaceship Vyom-9, its hull shining like liquid moonlight. Her teammates — Aarav, a coding genius, and Tara, a pilot-in-training — waved excitedly. “We’re really going to explore the Andhera Belt!” said Aarav.

The mission was clear: collect samples of rare lumina crystals from Planet Nivara — a distant world that shimmered blue in the telescope but had never been visited by humans. The crystals were said to store sunlight for centuries — a possible solution to Earth’s energy crisis.

As Vyom-9 lifted off, <name> pressed her face to the window. Earth shrank below — oceans, cities, forests, all turning into one tiny glowing dot. “Goodbye, Amma,” she whispered. “I’ll bring back a piece of light.”

Days passed in the hum of the spaceship — soft beeps, floating food packets, and glowing screens. But just as they neared Nivara, alarms blared. BEEP! BEEP! BEEP! “Power cells dropping!” Tara shouted. “The solar grid’s failing,” Aarav said, typing frantically. <name> noticed something strange — a cluster of bright lights outside the ship, moving in a pattern. “Wait — those aren’t stars!”

The glowing orbs floated closer, forming shapes that looked almost like… faces. Suddenly, a calm voice filled the cabin. “Travelers of light, do not fear.” The orbs shimmered, revealing small translucent creatures with eyes like stars. “We are the Lumari, guardians of Nivara. Why have you come?”

<name> took a deep breath. “Our planet is running out of energy. We seek your crystals — not to harm, but to learn.” The Lumari blinked slowly. “To take light, one must understand it. Solve our puzzle, and we will share it.” A swirling hologram appeared — a maze of stars connected by glowing lines.

“It’s a light path puzzle,” Aarav whispered. “Each line must shine without crossing twice.” <name> studied it carefully. “Wait… if we start from Nivara’s star and loop through the constellations shaped like ISRO’s logo…” She traced her finger through the glowing maze. The puzzle flared gold, and the Lumari cheered softly. “You see with heart and mind,” they said. “You may take one crystal — the Heart of Nivara.”

But just then, Vyom-9’s systems flickered again. “Power’s gone!” Tara yelled. The ship started drifting toward the planet’s surface. “We’re going to crash!” <name> clutched the glowing crystal. “Maybe it can power the ship!” She placed it gently into the reactor core. Light burst through the cabin — FWOOSH! — and the ship steadied. “Systems stable,” said the computer in a calm voice.

The Lumari smiled. “Light shared is never lost.” When Vyom-9 returned to Earth, scientists cheered. The crystal glowed softly in its containment field, enough to power an entire city. Reporters called <name> “India’s youngest space hero,” but she simply said, “I just listened — to the stars, to my friends, and to my heart.”

That night, back home under the same starry sky, <name> held the small Lumari pendant the aliens had gifted her. It shimmered faintly, as if whispering across galaxies. She smiled and whispered, “Thank you for the light.” Above, the stars twinkled — not just distant suns, but friends watching from afar.
